scenario: A law firm receives 200 contracts per week from different clients, each in a different template. Paralegals spend 4 hours per contract extracting key dates, obligations, and liability clauses.
why: Unstructured, varied documents requiring reading comprehension, entity extraction, and contextual judgment across formats. Agent with document understanding handles this at scale.

scenario: A property management company gets maintenance requests via email, WhatsApp, phone calls, and a web form. Each channel uses different language to describe the same problem.
why: Multi-channel unstructured input in natural language. An agent normalizes, classifies, and routes regardless of phrasing. Rules-based approach would need infinite branches.

scenario: A compliance officer at a bank spends 3 days per week reading regulatory updates from 12 different authorities to determine which ones affect their products.
why: High-volume text analysis requiring contextual understanding of which regulations apply to which products. Reading comprehension + domain reasoning at scale.

scenario: A municipality receives 15,000 citizen feedback submissions per year. They categorize them manually, and 40% are miscategorized.
why: Natural language classification at scale with nuance. Citizens describe issues in their own words. An agent handles ambiguity and context better than keyword rules.

scenario: A wholesale distributor's sales team needs to identify which of their 3,000 customers are likely to reduce orders next quarter, based on order history, email tone, and market news.
why: Multi-signal pattern recognition across structured and unstructured data. Combining order data, communication sentiment, and market context requires reasoning.

scenario: A construction company has 20 years of project post-mortems in PDF format. New project managers keep making the same mistakes because nobody reads the archives.
why: Institutional knowledge locked in unstructured documents. An agent can index, search, and surface relevant lessons learned contextually. RAG use case.

scenario: A customer service team handles product returns. Each case requires checking warranty terms, purchase date, damage photos, and return policy exceptions. It takes 25 minutes per case.
why: Multimodal (photos + text + policy documents) with decision logic that varies per case. Agent analyzes damage, cross-references terms, recommends decision.

scenario: A shipping company receives 500 booking requests per day via email, each containing different combinations of origin, destination, cargo type, weight, and special handling requirements buried in free text.
why: Structured data extraction from unstructured email at volume. Formats vary wildly. An agent parses, validates, and creates structured bookings.

scenario: An energy company monitors 2,000 wind turbines. Maintenance teams want to know which ones will fail next month based on vibration patterns, weather exposure, age, and service history.
why: Predictive maintenance combining sensor data, environmental factors, and historical patterns. Multi-variable reasoning over time-series data.

scenario: A government agency needs to redact personal information from 50,000 scanned documents before public release.
why: Scanned documents require OCR + entity recognition + contextual judgment about what constitutes PII in different contexts. Not a batch processing job; requires understanding.
